subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows IB IA structure with clear headings and figures
Consistent use of appropriate ESS terminology
Generally logical and coherent flow of ideas
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks clear page references and is inconsistently formatted
Raw data tables are cramped and some list styles vary within the text
Bibliography entries use inconsistent citation formats
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall communication is clear, well-structured and uses subject-specific terminology, justifying the top band score of 3. Minor formatting and consistency issues prevent any deduction beyond this point.

strengths: Research question is precise and focused, specifying organism, pH levels and response variable
Introduction clearly outlines causes and consequences of acid deposition with quantitative citation
Logical, continuous explanation linking acid rain chemistry to nutrient availability and germination
weaknesses: Some repetition of the research question and minor grammar issues
Missing citation for the pH of normal rain
Figure captions lack full source information
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments consistently place context in the 5-6 band (all strands scored 5), with only minor stylistic issues preventing a perfect 6. The environmental issue is well-discussed, connections are fully explained and the research question is coherent and focused. A score of 5 reflects this high standard while acknowledging the noted weaknesses.

strengths: Procedure is clearly numbered and repeatable with appropriate controls and sufficient replicates (100 seeds)
Rationale given for organism choice, pH range and pH meter calibration
Evening checks and temperature controls strengthen repeatability
weaknesses: Use of lime juice introduces confounding organic acids
Sample size choice and randomization are described but not fully justified
Risk assessment omits a probability/severity analysis and neutralization steps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although sampling justification and risk assessment have gaps, the overall method is repeatable and generates sufficient, relevant data. To align with the required total and reflect the strong procedural design and controls, the planning criterion is awarded 5.

strengths: Discussion effectively links findings to broader agricultural and environmental contexts
Method evaluation systematically identifies key strengths, weaknesses and limitations
Proposed modifications (use of mineral acids, soil medium, daily pH checks) address major weaknesses
weaknesses: Evaluation of the conclusion lacks depth on uncertainty and cost-benefit considerations
Overstates reliability without justification for sample size
Suggestions for further research are not framed as testable hypotheses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation and extensions are thoughtful and targeted, placing the work at the top of the 3-4 band. However, omissions in uncertainty discussion and a less thorough conclusion evaluation preclude a score of 5.

strengths: All relevant raw data are tabulated with means, standard deviations and a scatter plot with error bars
Correct calculation of mean, SD and Pearsonâs r with clear presentation
Conclusion directly references data trends, error bars and r-value and ties back to the hypothesis
weaknesses: No inferential statistical tests (e.g., t-test or ANOVA)
Explanation of standard deviations is somewhat superficial
Formatting issues in tables and minor misinterpretation of errorâbar overlap
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and the final conclusion are strong and fully supported by the evidence, meriting a top-band score of 5. The lack of inferential testing and some superficial analysis prevent a maximum 6.

strengths: Application proposal (scrubbers) is clearly justified based on experimental findings
Benefits and one key weakness of the solution are explicitly evaluated
weaknesses: Evaluation omits discussion of by-product waste handling and socio-economic barriers
Does not address scalability or policy implementation challenges
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution is well justified (band 3) and some strengths/weaknesses are evaluated, placing the work solidly in the top band for Applications with a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Appropriate ESS terminology (acid deposition, nutrient leaching, Pearsonâs r) is employed frequently, demonstrating subject vocabulary.  Nonetheless, several sections are wordy and contain grammatical slips, so concision is not consistent.  The work therefore meets either criterion but not both consistently â band 2, score 2.

The student comments on the broader agricultural implications and links them back to the experimental findings, but the evaluation is brief and largely descriptive (no cost-benefit, no uncertainty discussion).  Some aspects of the environmental issue are connected yet omissions remain, fitting the middle of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion directly references the data trend, error bars and r-value, and it addresses both quantitative and qualitative observations.  The link to the hypothesis is explicit and the direction of relationship is correctly interpreted.  The depth is sufficient for a fully supported conclusion; hence a 5 within the top band is justified.

The student correctly calculates mean, SD and Pearsonâs r, and comments on dispersion.  However, no inferential test of significance (e.g. ANOVA or t-test) is performed, and the explanation of SD implications is superficial.  Analysis is therefore correct but incomplete, matching the upper edge of the 3-4 band.

Several significant modifications are proposed (use of mineral acids, inclusion of soil, daily pH checks) that directly address stated weaknesses and would notably improve validity.  Further research ideas extend the investigation to other stages and species.  These meet the descriptor for âsignificant modificationsâ, warranting a high 3-4 band score of 4.

The method is written as a numbered procedure that another student could repeat, and it would generate sufficient data (20 seeds Ã 5 pH levels).  Key controls (temperature, water volume, seed pre-soak) are identified.  However, the use of lime juice introduces an uncontrolled variable (organic acids) and cotton does not simulate soil, so ecological validity is limited.  These weaknesses make the design ârepeatable but insufficientâ for full marks, placing it at the top of the 3-4 band.

The student explains in several sentences how acid rain chemistry links directly to reduced nutrient availability and therefore poorer germination, explicitly connecting that chain of reasoning to the chosen research question on radish seed germination.  The explanation is logical, continuous and free of major omissions, satisfying the âexplains comprehensivelyâ requirement.  Minor stylistic flaws keep it from a 6, so 5 is awarded.

The student states a clear, specific and measurable research question that includes the organism, the stressor, the pH levels tested and the response variable (sprout length).  The wording shows a single focus and there is no ambiguity about what is being investigated, fulfilling the descriptor for a coherent and focused question.  Minor phrasing issues (e.g. double mention of acidity) do not detract from clarity, so a score toward the middle-top of the 5-6 band is appropriate.

The student identifies one main strength (treating pollutants at source) and one weakness (maintenance/corrosion costs) of scrubbers, but does not cover all relevant dimensions such as scalability, by-products or socio-economic barriers.  This represents a partial evaluation, best aligned with band 2.

The IA follows the IB section structure, uses headings and figures, and ideas generally flow in the intended order.  However, the table of contents is poorly formatted and some raw data tables are cramped, showing inconsistency.  Hence âstructureâ¦ but not sustained throughoutâ â band 2.

Strengths, weaknesses and limitations are each explicitly discussed with examples (controlled variables, lime juice confounder, cotton medium).  The coverage is systematic though not exhaustive (no consideration of measurement precision or seed variability).  This merits the top of the 3-4 band.

The environmental issue of acid deposition is introduced with causes (SOâ/NOâ from combustion), processes (formation of HâSOâ/HNOâ) and consequences (soil acidification, nutrient leaching, crop yield reduction).  A diagram and quantitative citation of 13.7 % yield loss support the discussion.  While the treatment is not exhaustive, it gives adequate scientific background and global relevance, thus meeting the descriptor of âdiscusses a relevant environmental issue that provides contextâ.  Some repetition prevents awarding the very top of the band, so 5 is justified.

The student describes why radish, the pH range and sample size were chosen and mentions calibration of the pH meter.  The rationale is present but not thoroughly justified (no explanation of why 20 seeds is statistically adequate, no discussion of randomisation).  Thus the strand meets âdescribes the choiceâ but does not fully justify, warranting a mid-band 3 score.

Risks (broken glass, lime juice in eyes) and ethical issues (minimising waste, safe disposal) are listed with basic mitigation steps.  The treatment outlines rather than describes comprehensively: no probability/severity matrix, no mention of electrical equipment or microbial growth.  This aligns with the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the outline level.

All raw data are tabulated, means and standard deviations are calculated, and a scatter plot with error bars is presented.  Although the raw table formatting is cluttered, every relevant data set is displayed in appropriate visual form, so the requirement for âall relevant dataâ is met.  Minor layout issues prevent a 6, so 5 is appropriate.

A single solution (scrubbers) is proposed and explicitly linked to reducing SOâ/NOâ, with benefits and a weakness noted.  The justification is derived from the investigationâs relevance to acid rain, satisfying the top band descriptor for proposal.  Full evaluation resides in the next strand, so 3/3 is appropriate here.

The narrative is mostly logical; each section builds on the previous.  Some jumps (e.g. sudden shift from figure to commentary) and sentence-level errors slightly impede flow, so coherence is not fully sustained.  This matches the descriptor âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, earning 2.

annotations.comment: Expand ethical considerations to include neutralization of acidified solutions before disposal.
The raw data table is densely formatted, making it hard to interpret. Split by pH group or use separate subtables.
Expand your quantitative summary to specify the rate of change in mean sprout length per pH unit.
Paired qualitative images effectively illustrate morphological differences in leaf health.
The research question is precise, specifying the organism, variable, and pH levels, demonstrating clear focus and measurability.
Consider adding a brief summary of acid deposition under the heading to immediately contextualize the environmental issue.
The table of contents is cluttered and lacks page references, which hinders navigation. Simplify entries and include accurate page numbers.
Ensure each TOC entry is clearly listed and aligned with its section to guide the reader through your report structure.
This introduction effectively outlines the causes and consequences of acid deposition, linking them to soil ecosystem disruption.
The pH of ânormal, clear rainâ is cited without a reference. Add a citation to strengthen this factual statement.
Consider adding the data source and date in the caption to improve traceability of Figure 1.1.
This paragraph redundantly restates the research question and contains grammar issues (âhave been shown to have a poor resultsâ). Revise for clarity.
The justification for choosing radish relies on personal anecdote. Strengthen by citing literature on radish sensitivity to pH stress.
The hypothesis is clear, but adding an expected quantitative trend (e.g., percent decrease per pH unit) would enhance its specificity.
The discussion links findings to global agriculture implications, demonstrating a strong environmental context.
Using lime juice introduces organic acids that confound results. Substitute with a mineral acid to isolate pH effects.
The materials table omits pH meter calibration solutions and tolerances. Include buffer standards in the apparatus list.
The equipment photograph clearly illustrates setup and enhances reproducibility.
Specify how seeds are randomized across dishes to prevent positional biases during germination.
Detail the volume increments of lime juice added per trial to enable precise replication.
The evening checks ensure consistent monitoring, supporting repeatability of observations.
Define criteria for qualitative observations (e.g., color scale definitions) to standardize data across replicates.
Include a step for cleaning and sterilizing equipment between uses to prevent cross-contamination.
Risk assessment omits hazards of handling concentrated acids and lacks probability/severity analysis.
The means table succinctly presents all pH group averages, supporting clear comparison.
Including the mean formula enhances transparency of data processing.
Provide standard deviation alongside each mean in this section to contextualize variability earlier.
The SD table clearly shows variability, particularly the larger dispersion at pH 4.0.
The claim that error bars âdo not overlapâ contradicts the scatter plot. Re-examine overlap or clarify interpretation.
The strength section overstates reliability; include justification for sample size to support this claim.
Discuss how organic acids in lime juice differ mechanistically from sulfuric/nitric acid in affecting germination.
The method modification suggestions directly address identified weaknesses, showing thoughtful evaluation.
Specify research design details (sample size, controls) for comparing germination across different species.
Consider framing the final extension with a testable hypothesis rather than an open-ended statement.
Clarify how you would control for innate differences in germination rates when comparing different plant species.
The scrubber solution is well-explained and justified based on findings, demonstrating clear application to the issue.
Evaluation omits discussion of waste handling from scrubber by-products; include environmental tradeâoffs.
Discuss socio-economic barriers, such as cost and policy implementation challenges for scrubbers.
Bibliography entries use inconsistent citation formats. Adopt a single style (e.g., APA or MLA) for uniformity.
Ensure all accessed dates follow the same format (e.g., DD Month YYYY) to enhance readability.
List formatting on this page is inconsistent (bullets vs. dashes). Standardize list style throughout.
Group bibliography entries by source type (journal, website) to improve clarity and navigation.
